text: Dense adhesions are visible in the pelvis, with small bowel loops herniating through the pelvic outlet. A biologic mesh is observed as part of the primary repair of the pelvic floor. Dynamic retractors and table assist are utilized to enhance exposure of the operative field.

The surgeon performs blunt and sharp dissection using monopolar scissors and bipolar forceps to mobilize small bowel loops, carefully avoiding serosal tears. Energy use is minimized to prevent contamination of the operative field, which could affect the suitability of non-absorbable mesh.

The final view of the operative field shows the peritoneal bladder flap secured posteriorly to the sacrum with interrupted absorbable stitches. The dissection continues over the bladder in the rectus space, with bilateral exposure of the Cooper's ligaments.

The surgeon mobilizes the bladder peritoneal flap laterally to provide tissue for posterior fixation. The defect is measured intracorporeally, and a 50 by 20 centimeter coated non-absorbable mesh is positioned at the pelvic inlet. The mesh is fixed with non-absorbable stitches to the Cooper's ligaments bilaterally on the anterior aspect for secure fixation.

The mesh is secured posteriorly at the sacral promontory using absorbable stitches and cyanocrylate glue. The surgeon ensures adequate overlap of the mesh on its lateral aspects to prevent recurrence.
The patient is positioned prone for skin closure using interrupted non-absorbable stitches. A surgical drain is visibly left in place at the end of the procedure.
